title: How to Maintain Bone & Joint Health During Winters
excerpt: Cold weather can worsen arthritis and joint pain. Learn how to protect your bones and joints during winter with expert advice from our orthopaedic team.
content:
As temperatures drop, many patients with arthritis and joint problems report increased pain and stiffness. This is not merely psychological — cold weather causes muscles and tendons to contract, reducing blood flow to joints and making them less flexible.

## Why Winter Affects Joints

Barometric pressure changes that accompany cold fronts can cause joints to expand slightly, increasing pain in already inflamed areas. Additionally, people tend to be less active in winter, leading to muscle weakening that puts more stress on joints.

## Key Strategies for Bone & Joint Health

**1. Stay Warm**
Layer your clothing and pay special attention to knees, hips, and hands — joints that are particularly vulnerable to cold. Thermal undergarments and joint warmers can make a significant difference.

**2. Stay Active**
Indoor exercises like swimming in a heated pool, yoga, or stationary cycling maintain joint mobility without the cold exposure. Even 30 minutes of daily gentle movement can dramatically reduce stiffness.

**3. Vitamin D Supplementation**
Sunlight exposure drops in winter, reducing your body's natural Vitamin D production. Vitamin D is crucial for calcium absorption and bone health. Consult your doctor about appropriate supplementation — typically 1,000–2,000 IU daily for most adults.

**4. Anti-Inflammatory Diet**
Foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids (salmon, walnuts, flaxseeds), turmeric, and ginger have natural anti-inflammatory properties. Reduce processed foods and sugars that can worsen inflammation.

**5. Physiotherapy**
If you experience significant seasonal pain, a course of physiotherapy can strengthen the muscles around your joints, providing better support and reducing pain naturally.

**When to See a Doctor**
Seek medical advice if joint pain is severe, limiting daily activities, accompanied by swelling or redness, or if you notice joint deformity. Early intervention prevents long-term damage.

At NexusAI Hospital, our orthopaedic team provides comprehensive joint care from conservative management to advanced surgical solutions. Book a consultation to develop your personalised winter joint care plan.
author: Dr. Ramneek Mahajan

title: Robot-Assisted Kidney Transplant: The Future of Transplant Surgery
excerpt: Our team recently performed the first robot-assisted kidney transplant in the region. Discover how this groundbreaking procedure reduces recovery time and improves outcomes.
content:
The world of transplant surgery has been transformed by robotic technology, and NexusAI Hospital is at the forefront of this revolution. Our nephrology and urology team, led by Dr. Anant Kumar, recently performed the first robot-assisted kidney transplant in the region — a milestone that represents the future of transplant medicine.

## What Is Robot-Assisted Kidney Transplant?

Traditional kidney transplantation requires a large incision (15–20 cm) in the lower abdomen. Robot-assisted transplantation, performed using the da Vinci Surgical System, allows surgeons to make smaller incisions (typically 5–8 cm) while maintaining superior visibility and precision through high-definition 3D imaging.

## Advantages Over Traditional Surgery

**Smaller Incisions**
The minimally invasive approach reduces surgical trauma, leading to less post-operative pain and scarring.

**Faster Recovery**
Patients typically return home within 3–4 days compared to 7–10 days for open surgery. Return to normal activities is possible within 3–4 weeks rather than 6–8 weeks.

**Reduced Complication Risk**
Lower rates of wound infections, hernia, and lymphocele (lymph fluid collection) — common complications of open transplant surgery.

**Better Outcomes for Obese Patients**
Previously, obese patients were considered poor candidates for kidney transplantation due to surgical risks. Robotic transplantation makes this option viable for a much broader patient population.

## The Procedure at NexusAI

Our state-of-the-art da Vinci Xi Surgical System allows Dr. Kumar to operate with millimetre precision from a console, translating hand movements into precise robotic actions. The procedure typically takes 3–4 hours and is performed under general anaesthesia.

## Patient Selection

Not all patients are suitable candidates for robotic transplantation. Our transplant team conducts a thorough assessment including kidney function tests, cardiac evaluation, and imaging to determine the optimal surgical approach for each individual.

If you or a loved one is awaiting kidney transplantation, contact our transplant centre to discuss whether robotic surgery might be right for you.
author: Dr. Anant Kumar

title: Body Weight — What You Must Know About BMI and Metabolic Health
excerpt: BMI is just one piece of the puzzle. Our nutrition specialist explains the comprehensive approach to understanding and managing healthy body weight.
content:
Body weight is one of the most discussed yet frequently misunderstood aspects of health. While BMI (Body Mass Index) provides a useful screening tool, it tells only part of the story. Here is what you truly need to know about body weight and metabolic health.

## Beyond BMI: Understanding Body Composition

BMI is calculated by dividing weight (kg) by height squared (m²). A BMI of 18.5–24.9 is considered normal, 25–29.9 overweight, and 30+ obese. However, BMI does not distinguish between muscle and fat mass.

A bodybuilder may have a BMI of 28 and be in excellent health, while someone with a "normal" BMI of 23 may carry dangerous visceral fat around their organs — a condition called TOFI (Thin Outside, Fat Inside).

## Metabolic Health Markers That Matter

Instead of fixating on the scale, focus on these metabolic health indicators:

- **Waist circumference**: Men <90 cm, Women <80 cm (Indian standards)
- **Fasting blood glucose**: <100 mg/dL
- **Blood pressure**: <120/80 mmHg
- **Triglycerides**: <150 mg/dL
- **HDL cholesterol**: Men >40 mg/dL, Women >50 mg/dL

A person is considered metabolically healthy when all these markers are in the normal range without medication.

## The Indian Metabolic Context

Indians are at higher metabolic risk at lower BMI levels compared to Western populations. The "Asian cutoffs" recommend action at BMI >23 for overweight and >27.5 for obesity in Indian populations. This is because South Asians tend to carry more abdominal fat and have a higher predisposition to insulin resistance.

## Sustainable Weight Management

**Caloric balance** is the foundation, but the quality of calories matters enormously. Focus on:
- Protein at every meal (0.8–1.2g per kg body weight)
- Fibre-rich vegetables and whole grains
- Limiting ultra-processed foods
- Adequate hydration (2–3 litres daily)

**Physical activity** — both aerobic exercise and strength training — improves insulin sensitivity, preserves muscle mass during weight loss, and maintains metabolic rate.

**Sleep and stress management** profoundly affect weight. Chronic sleep deprivation increases ghrelin (hunger hormone) and decreases leptin (satiety hormone), making weight management dramatically harder.

For personalised nutritional guidance tailored to your metabolic profile, consult our nutrition specialists at NexusAI Hospital.
author: Dr. Vandana Soni

title: Understanding Heart Disease: Prevention is Better Than Cure
excerpt: Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of death worldwide. Learn the key risk factors, warning signs, and prevention strategies from our cardiology team.
content:
Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is the leading cause of death globally, accounting for 17.9 million lives annually. In India, CVD is responsible for 28% of all deaths, and alarmingly, Indians develop heart disease 10 years earlier than Western populations. The good news: up to 80% of premature heart disease is preventable.

## Know Your Risk Factors

**Modifiable risk factors** (those you can control):
- High blood pressure (hypertension)
- High cholesterol
- Smoking and tobacco use
- Diabetes
- Obesity and physical inactivity
- Unhealthy diet
- Excessive alcohol consumption
- Chronic stress

**Non-modifiable risk factors**:
- Age (risk increases after 45 for men, 55 for women)
- Family history of heart disease
- Gender (men have higher risk until women reach menopause)
- Ethnicity (South Asians have higher risk)

## Warning Signs You Should Never Ignore

Heart attacks do not always present dramatically. Know these symptoms:
- Chest pain, pressure, tightness, or discomfort
- Pain radiating to arm, shoulder, neck, jaw, or back
- Shortness of breath
- Nausea, cold sweats, or lightheadedness
- Unusual fatigue (especially in women)

Women often experience atypical symptoms — nausea, jaw pain, and extreme fatigue without chest pain. Do not dismiss these signs.

## Prevention Strategy

Our cardiologists recommend the ABCDE approach:
- **A**: Aspirin therapy (as recommended by your doctor)
- **B**: Blood pressure control
- **C**: Cholesterol management
- **D**: Diet and Diabetes management
- **E**: Exercise regularly

Aim for 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity or 75 minutes of vigorous activity weekly. Mediterranean or DASH diet patterns show strong evidence for cardiovascular protection.

Schedule your cardiac health check at NexusAI Hospital today — our Happy Heart package provides comprehensive cardiac screening for just ₹4,500.
author: Dr. Priya Mehta

title: Childhood Vaccines: A Complete Guide for Parents
excerpt: Vaccinations are one of the most effective public health interventions ever developed. Our paediatrician answers the most common questions parents have about childhood immunisation.
content:
Few medical interventions have saved as many lives as vaccines. Since widespread vaccination began, diseases like smallpox have been eradicated and polio is nearly eliminated globally. Yet vaccine hesitancy remains a significant public health challenge. As a paediatrician, I want to address the most common concerns parents bring to my clinic.

## The Indian Immunisation Schedule

The Government of India's National Immunisation Schedule (NIS) provides free vaccines against 12 diseases. The recommended schedule begins at birth with BCG, OPV, and Hepatitis B vaccines.

**Key vaccines and timing:**
- **Birth**: BCG, OPV-0, Hepatitis B-1
- **6 weeks**: DTwP/DTaP, IPV, Hib, Hepatitis B-2, Rotavirus
- **10 weeks**: DTwP/DTaP, IPV, Hib, Rotavirus
- **14 weeks**: DTwP/DTaP, IPV, Hib, Hepatitis B-3, Rotavirus
- **9 months**: Measles/MR
- **12 months**: Typhoid conjugate vaccine
- **15 months**: MMR, Varicella, Hepatitis A
- **16–18 months**: DTwP/DTaP booster, IPV booster

## Common Parent Questions

**"Are multiple vaccines at once safe?"**
Absolutely. A baby's immune system can handle thousands of antigens simultaneously. Giving vaccines together reduces the number of clinic visits and ensures timely protection.

**"Do vaccines cause autism?"**
This myth originated from a fraudulent 1998 study that has been thoroughly debunked and retracted. Dozens of large-scale studies involving millions of children have found no link between vaccines and autism.

**"Natural immunity is better than vaccine immunity"**
For some diseases, natural infection does produce strong immunity — but at the cost of experiencing the disease, which carries real risks of complications, hospitalisation, and death. Vaccines provide immunity safely.

**"My child had a mild fever after vaccination — is this normal?"**
Yes. Mild fever, soreness at the injection site, and irritability for 1–2 days are normal immune responses showing the vaccine is working. Paracetamol can be given for comfort.

Bring your child's vaccination card to every clinic visit. Our paediatric team at NexusAI is happy to review your child's immunisation history and ensure they are fully protected.
author: Dr. Harshil Patel

title: Stroke Warning Signs: Act FAST to Save a Life
excerpt: Every 40 seconds, someone in the world has a stroke. Recognising the warning signs and acting immediately can mean the difference between full recovery and permanent disability.
content:
Stroke is a medical emergency where every minute matters. When a stroke occurs, approximately 1.9 million neurons die every minute the brain goes without blood supply. The saying in neurology is true: "Time is brain."

## The FAST Test

Remember the FAST acronym to identify stroke symptoms:

- **F — Face drooping**: Ask the person to smile. Does one side droop?
- **A — Arm weakness**: Ask them to raise both arms. Does one drift downward?
- **S — Speech difficulty**: Ask them to repeat a simple phrase. Is their speech slurred or strange?
- **T — Time to call emergency**: If you see ANY of these signs, call emergency services immediately.

## Additional Warning Signs

Beyond FAST, watch for:
- Sudden severe headache with no known cause ("thunderclap headache")
- Sudden confusion or trouble understanding
- Sudden vision problems in one or both eyes
- Sudden dizziness, loss of balance, or lack of coordination

## Types of Stroke

**Ischaemic stroke** (87% of strokes): Caused by a blood clot blocking a brain artery. Treatment with clot-dissolving medication (tPA) must occur within 4.5 hours of symptom onset — this is why rapid response is critical.

**Haemorrhagic stroke** (13% of strokes): Caused by a ruptured blood vessel. Treatment involves controlling bleeding and reducing brain pressure.

**TIA (Transient Ischaemic Attack)**: Often called a "mini-stroke," a TIA causes temporary stroke-like symptoms that resolve within 24 hours. Do NOT ignore a TIA — it is a serious warning sign that a major stroke may be imminent. Seek emergency care immediately.

## Risk Factor Management

High blood pressure is the single most important modifiable risk factor for stroke. Control blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg through medication, diet, and exercise. Other key risk factors include atrial fibrillation, diabetes, high cholesterol, smoking, and obesity.

## NexusAI Stroke Centre

Our dedicated Stroke Centre provides 24/7 emergency stroke care, including advanced neuroimaging and thrombolysis. Our neurointervention team performs mechanical thrombectomy — a procedure to physically remove blood clots from brain arteries — achieving outcomes among the best in the region.

Know the signs. Act FAST. Save a brain.
author: Dr. Sahil Achhava
